text: The default mode network has a troubling vulnerability: smartphones exploit it. When the network runs its natural program — weaving autobiographical memory, imagining others' mental states, planning — it requires uninterrupted time. Psychologist Mary Helen Immordino-Yang found that adolescents who rarely experienced quiet, inward-facing downtime showed stunted development in the DMN's social-cognition functions. The network needs the same conditions that seem boring to us: no notifications, no scrolling, no ambient input. Silence is not emptiness for the brain. It is the operating condition under which the most distinctly human cognitive work gets done.
question: What did Mary Helen Immordino-Yang find in adolescents who rarely experienced quiet downtime?

options.text: They performed better on standardized tests but worse on social tasks.
They showed stunted development in the DMN's social-cognition functions.
Their default mode networks became overactive and caused anxiety.
They developed stronger task-positive networks to compensate.
